summary: Jeff Atwood announces his new venture with Joel Spolsky: Stack Overflow, a Q&A site for programmers. He explains his motivation for leaving his job was to build something meaningful, not just blog full-time, citing industry role models who both write and build. Despite past public criticism of Spolsky, Atwood emphasizes that disagreement is healthy and expresses admiration for Joel's approach to building a sustainable programmer-friendly company. The goal is to unlock programming knowledge trapped in forums, help files, and unread books, making it easily searchable and accessible to all programmers regardless of their technology stack.
key_points: Atwood founded Stack Overflow with Joel Spolsky despite having publicly criticized him in the past, demonstrating that professional collaboration doesn't require complete agreement
The world's best tech voices (Paul Graham, Joel Spolsky, etc.) share one trait: they build things, not just write about them
Stack Overflow's mission is to 'collectively increase the sum total of good programming knowledge in the world'
The site was positioned as the anti-Experts-Exchange: accessible, clean, and without the sleazy search engine gaming
Good programming knowledge is trapped in forums, help files, and books nobody reads anymore, and Stack Overflow aims to liberate it
money_quotes: The world has enough vapid commentary blogs. I want to build stuff – and talk about it.
I distrust people who agree with me all the time.
If you agree with everything I write here, why would you bother reading? At that point, we're the same person.
There's far too much great programming information trapped in forums, buried in online help, or hidden away in books that nobody buys any more.
Stackoverflow is sort of like the anti-experts-exchange (minus the nausea-inducing sleaze and quasi-legal search engine gaming) meets wikipedia meets programming reddit.
I refuse to become a full-time blogger. I think that's a cop-out.
key_insight: The most respected voices in tech don't just write—they build things, and the best programming knowledge needs to be liberated from obscure corners of the internet and made universally accessible.
